database_explained.what_is_rls: Row Level Security (RLS) is a PostgreSQL feature that filters which database rows a user can read or write, enforced at the database level — not in your app code.
database_explained.why_rls_matters: Even if a bug in your code tries to fetch another user's data, RLS blocks it at the database. Every table has policies like: 'user can only SELECT rows where user_id = auth.uid()'

tables.purpose: Stores additional info about each landlord user beyond what Supabase Auth stores

columns.purpose: Matches auth.users.id exactly — this is the link between Auth and this table
columns.beginner_explanation: Same ID as the user's Supabase Auth account. Used to join the two tables together.

columns.purpose: Landlord's full name, shown in sidebar
columns.beginner_explanation: Simple text field. Filled in on signup or editable in Settings.
Optional field stored as text, not validated as a phone number format.

columns.purpose: Property management company name if applicable
columns.beginner_explanation: Optional — shown on lease documents.

columns.purpose: URL to profile picture (from Google OAuth or uploaded)
columns.beginner_explanation: If user signed in with Google, this gets Google's profile picture URL automatically.
Default: UTC. User can change in Settings > Preferences.

columns.purpose: Default currency for rent amounts (USD, GBP, AUD...)
columns.beginner_explanation: Just a display preference — the app shows $ or £ symbols based on this setting.
Currently informational. Could gate features behind plan checks in the future.

columns.purpose: JSON object of notification preferences: rent_due, maintenance_update, lease_expiry, payment_received
columns.beginner_explanation: JSONB lets you store a whole object in one column. Example: {rent_due: true, lease_expiry: false}

tables.connects_to: auth.users (via id), indirectly to all other tables via user_id
tables.rls_policy: Users can only read and update their own profile row (WHERE id = auth.uid())
tables.beginner_explanation: Every landlord who signs up gets one row in this table. It's like a user settings table.

tables.purpose: Stores each rental property owned by a landlord

columns.beginner_explanation: Auto-generated unique ID. Used as a foreign key in tenants, expenses, maintenance_tickets, leases.

columns.purpose: The landlord who owns this property. References auth.users.id
columns.beginner_explanation: This is how RLS works — every query filters by user_id = auth.uid() so landlords only see their own properties.
A friendly name chosen by the landlord. Not the full address.
Required field.
Optional but useful for filtering.

columns.purpose: Country (United States, United Kingdom, Australia, etc.)
columns.beginner_explanation: Used to determine relevant regulations and currency defaults.
Stored lowercase.

columns.purpose: Default monthly rent for this property
columns.beginner_explanation: When a new tenant is added, their rent_amount defaults to this value.

columns.purpose: Currency for this property's rent (USD, GBP, AUD...)
columns.beginner_explanation: Can differ per property — a landlord might own properties in two countries.
Shows as a colored badge on the property card.

tables.beginner_explanation: The central table of the app. Almost every other table references a property via property_id.

tables.purpose: Stores each tenant renting a property

columns.beginner_explanation: Referenced by rent_payments, leases, maintenance_tickets.

columns.purpose: The landlord who manages this tenant
columns.beginner_explanation: RLS filter: landlords only see their own tenants.

columns.purpose: Which property this tenant lives in
columns.beginner_explanation: Foreign key to properties.id.
Required field.

columns.purpose: Tenant's email — used to send portal invite
columns.beginner_explanation: When landlord clicks 'Invite to Portal', this email receives the invitation.
Optional contact info.
Can differ from property's default rent_amount — useful for multi-unit buildings.
Only active tenants get rent_payment rows auto-generated each month.
For reference only — doesn't trigger any automation.

columns.purpose: Hex color for the tenant's avatar initials circle
columns.beginner_explanation: Randomly assigned on creation from a palette of colors. Makes the UI look varied and easy to scan.

columns.purpose: The Supabase Auth user ID created for this tenant's portal account
columns.beginner_explanation: This is the critical field for the tenant portal! When a tenant is invited, a new Supabase auth user is created and their ID is stored here. The useTenantData hook queries WHERE portal_user_id = auth.uid().

tables.rls_policy: Landlords: SELECT/UPDATE/DELETE where user_id = auth.uid(). Tenants (portal): SELECT where portal_user_id = auth.uid()
tables.beginner_explanation: Tenant portal data isolation works through portal_user_id. Each tenant can only see their OWN row because the RLS policy checks their auth user ID against portal_user_id.

tables.purpose: One row per tenant per month representing their rent obligation and payment status

columns.beginner_explanation: Auto-generated.
RLS filter.

columns.purpose: Which tenant this payment is for
columns.beginner_explanation: Foreign key to tenants.id.
Denormalized for query convenience.
Copied from tenant.rent_amount when the row is auto-generated.

columns.purpose: First day of the month the rent is due
columns.beginner_explanation: Auto-set to the 1st of the current month when rows are generated.

columns.purpose: pending or paid (overdue is computed at display time, not stored)
columns.beginner_explanation: Overdue is NOT stored in the database. It is calculated in JavaScript: if status is not paid AND due_date < today, it is displayed as overdue.

columns.purpose: Date the landlord confirmed payment was received
columns.beginner_explanation: Set when landlord clicks 'Mark Paid' and picks a date.
Free text field shown to landlord only.

tables.rls_policy: Landlords: all operations where user_id = auth.uid(). Tenants: SELECT only where tenant_id in (SELECT id FROM tenants WHERE portal_user_id = auth.uid())
tables.beginner_explanation: Auto-generation logic: When the rent page loads for a month, the app checks which active tenants do NOT have a row for that month yet and inserts them automatically.

tables.purpose: Tracks property maintenance issues and repair requests

columns.beginner_explanation: Used in the /dashboard/maintenance/[id] dynamic route.
RLS filter.
Foreign key.
Null if landlord raised the ticket themselves.
Required field shown on card.
Optional longer text.

columns.purpose: plumbing, electrical, painting, structural, appliance, general, other
columns.beginner_explanation: Stored lowercase. Used for filtering.
Shown as colored indicator on card — red for high, yellow for medium.
Updated by landlord. Tabs on the maintenance page filter by this.
If tenant, shows an orange 'Tenant' badge on the card.

columns.purpose: Path to uploaded receipt in Supabase Storage receipts bucket
columns.beginner_explanation: Just the file path, not a full URL. A signed URL is generated on demand.
When filled in, appears as a cost indicator. Resolving the ticket auto-creates an expense.

tables.rls_policy: Landlords: all operations where user_id = auth.uid(). Tenants: SELECT where property_id in tenant's property, INSERT where raised_by = tenant
tables.beginner_explanation: When a maintenance ticket is resolved with a cost, the detail page automatically inserts a new row into the expenses table — linking maintenance to financial records.

tables.purpose: Tracks all property-related costs and expenditures

columns.beginner_explanation: Auto-generated.
RLS filter.

columns.purpose: Which property the expense is for (nullable)

Required.
Stored as decimal number.

columns.purpose: plumbing, electrical, cleaning, insurance, utilities, management fee, legal, other, general repair
columns.beginner_explanation: Stored lowercase. Used for color coding and PDF grouping.
Defaults to today when creating a new expense.
Optional. User can upload a photo of the receipt.
Free text for anything extra.

tables.beginner_explanation: The PDF export feature uses jsPDF + jspdf-autotable to generate a formatted table of all visible expenses (after filters applied) as a downloadable PDF.

tables.purpose: Stores rental agreements between landlord and tenant with digital signing workflow

columns.beginner_explanation: Auto-generated.
RLS filter.
Foreign key.
Foreign key.
Required.

columns.purpose: Lease end date — drives expiry countdown logic
columns.beginner_explanation: Days remaining = ceil((end_date - today) / 86400000). Shown in red if < 30 days, yellow if < 60.

columns.purpose: Monthly rent specified in this lease
columns.beginner_explanation: Can differ from current tenant.rent_amount — the lease locks in the rent at signing time.

columns.purpose: Path to uploaded lease PDF in lease-documents bucket
columns.beginner_explanation: Optional PDF the landlord uploads as the official lease document.

columns.purpose: Base64 image data of landlord's drawn signature
columns.beginner_explanation: react-signature-canvas captures the signature as a PNG data URL which is stored in this text column.

columns.purpose: Base64 image data of tenant's drawn signature
columns.beginner_explanation: Same as landlord_signature but filled by the tenant via the /tenant/lease/sign page.

tables.rls_policy: Landlords: all operations where user_id = auth.uid(). Tenants: SELECT and partial update (signature only) where tenant_id in their tenant record.
tables.beginner_explanation: The expiry warning banner on the leases page uses a client-side calculation: filter leases where status='signed' AND days_left is between 0 and 60.

tables.purpose: Records significant actions for compliance and debugging (accessed via lib/audit.ts)

columns.beginner_explanation: Auto-generated.
Can be landlord or system (cron job).
Free text describing what happened.

columns.purpose: Additional context as JSON (old status, new status, tenant email, etc)
columns.beginner_explanation: JSONB lets you store flexible data without needing fixed columns.
For security audit trail.
Auto-set by Supabase default value.

tables.rls_policy: INSERT only (service role writes, users cannot read their own audit log via client)
tables.beginner_explanation: Every sensitive action like inviting a tenant or changing a lease status gets logged here. Good practice for any production app dealing with financial data.

database_explained.how_tables_relate: properties is the root table. tenants belong to a property. rent_payments, maintenance_tickets, leases, and expenses all have both a user_id (landlord) and property_id (property). This double reference means you can query by either landlord OR property. The tenant portal uses portal_user_id on the tenants table to link a Supabase auth account to exactly one tenant record.

step_by_step: 1. User fills in email + password on /signup page
2. supabase.auth.signUp({ email, password }) is called
3. Supabase creates a new row in auth.users
4. Supabase sends a confirmation email (if email confirmation is enabled in Supabase dashboard)
5. User is redirected to /dashboard
6. The useProfile hook runs, tries to fetch profile row, finds none, upserts a blank profiles row
signup_flow.supabase_method_used: supabase.auth.signUp({ email, password, options: { data: { full_name } } })
signup_flow.what_happens_in_database: A row is created in auth.users (managed by Supabase). A row is upserted into profiles with matching id.

step_by_step: 1. User enters email + password on /login
2. supabase.auth.signInWithPassword({ email, password }) called
3. Supabase validates credentials and returns a session token
4. Session is stored in cookies by @supabase/ssr automatically
5. Router pushes to /dashboard
6. middleware.ts sees the session cookie on next request and allows access
1. User clicks 'Continue with Google' button
2. supabase.auth.signInWithOAuth({ provider: 'google', options: { redirectTo: '/auth/callback' } }) is called
3. User is redirected to Google's consent screen
4. After approval, Google redirects to /auth/callback?code=...
5. app/auth/callback/route.ts exchanges the code for a session via supabase.auth.exchangeCodeForSession(code)
6. Session is set, user is redirected to /dashboard
setup_required: Enable Google provider in Supabase Dashboard > Authentication > Providers
Create OAuth credentials in Google Cloud Console
Add your Supabase callback URL to Google's Authorized Redirect URIs
Add your production URL to Supabase > Authentication > URL Configuration

middleware_protection.what_it_does: Intercepts every HTTP request before it reaches any page, checks for a valid Supabase session, and redirects unauthorized users
middleware_protection.how_it_works: Creates a Supabase server client from request cookies. Calls supabase.auth.getSession(). If pathname starts with /dashboard and no session → redirect to /login. If pathname starts with /tenant (except public auth pages) and no session → redirect to /tenant/login.
middleware_protection.beginner_explanation: The matcher config at the bottom of middleware.ts tells Next.js which URLs to run the middleware on. It runs on everything except static files (_next/static, images, etc.).

session_management.how_session_persists: Supabase stores the auth token in browser cookies. @supabase/ssr handles reading/writing cookies in both server components and client components. The middleware refreshes the session on every request to keep it alive.
session_management.how_to_get_current_user: Client components: const { data: { user } } = await supabase.auth.getUser(). Server components: const supabase = await createClient() from lib/supabase/server.ts, then supabase.auth.getUser()
session_management.beginner_explanation: You never store the user ID yourself — Supabase manages it. Any time you need to know who is logged in, you call supabase.auth.getUser() and it reads the cookie for you. Never use getSession() for security-sensitive operations; always use getUser() as it verifies the token with the server.

tenant_portal_explained.how_it_is_separate: The tenant portal lives at /tenant/* routes with its own layout (app/tenant/layout.tsx) that has different navigation, different styling (light mode only, warm background), and completely different data access patterns. Landlord has no access to /tenant/* routes and vice versa.

step_by_step: 1. Landlord goes to tenant detail page /dashboard/tenants/[id]
2. Sees 'Tenant Portal Access' card with an Invite button
3. Clicks Invite — frontend calls POST /api/tenant/invite with { tenantId }
4. API route (app/api/tenant/invite/route.ts) uses the SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY to call supabase.auth.admin.createUser({ email, password: randomPassword, email_confirm: true })
5. The new Supabase auth user ID is saved to tenants.portal_user_id
6. sendTenantPortalInvite() from lib/email/resend.ts sends an email with a link to /tenant/set-password
7. Tenant opens email, clicks link, sets their own password
8. Tenant can now log in at /tenant/login

portal_user_id_explained.what_it_is: A UUID column on the tenants table that stores the Supabase auth user ID created for that tenant's portal account
portal_user_id_explained.why_needed: Tenants log into the portal as Supabase auth users, but the app needs to know WHICH tenant record in the tenants table belongs to that logged-in user. portal_user_id is that bridge.
portal_user_id_explained.how_it_gets_set: Set by the /api/tenant/invite API route after successfully creating the auth user: supabase.from('tenants').update({ portal_user_id: newUser.id }).eq('id', tenantId)

data_isolation.how_it_works: The useTenantData hook queries: supabase.from('tenants').select('*, properties(*)').eq('portal_user_id', user.id).single(). The RLS policy on tenants allows tenants to SELECT only rows where portal_user_id = auth.uid(). This means each tenant can only ever see their own record — and from that, can access their linked property, rent payments, and lease.

what_are_the_3_states: undefined — session is still being checked (initial state, show blank loading screen)
Session object — authenticated (render the portal UI)
auth_session_3_states.why_3_states_needed: If you only check for null vs session, there's a flash where null appears temporarily before the session loads — causing a redirect to login even for logged-in users. The undefined state means 'wait, still loading.'
auth_session_3_states.how_implemented: const [session, setSession] = useState<Session | null | undefined>(undefined). Supabase getSession() is async — until it resolves, state stays undefined. Once resolved, it becomes either null or a Session object.

files: app/tenant/layout.tsx — portal shell with nav and 3-state auth
app/tenant/set-password/page.tsx — first-time password setup from invite link
hooks/useTenantData.ts — fetches tenant record for logged-in portal user
components/tenant/NoTenantFound.tsx — shown when portal_user_id not matched
